explanation:
**Normal health values** come from large population studies (NHANES, WHO, CDC). They vary by age, sex, ethnicity, and region. They are guidelines; your personal medical history always takes precedence.

## How it's calculated

600-800 IU/day depending on age. 15-20 minutes of midday sun.

This calculator applies the formula with the values you enter, checking typical ranges and alerting you to unusual values.

## Normal reference values

| Parameter | Normal Adult Range |
|---|---|
| Blood Pressure | <120/80 mmHg |
| Resting Heart Rate | 60-100 bpm |
| Body Temperature | 36.1-37.2°C |
| SpO2 (Blood Oxygen) | 95-100% |
| Fasting Blood Glucose | 70-100 mg/dL |
| HbA1c | <5.7% |
| Total Cholesterol | <200 mg/dL |
| LDL Cholesterol | <100 mg/dL |
| HDL Cholesterol | >40 (M) / >50 (F) |

## Final Notes

This calculation is a guidance reference only. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult with a qualified professional. Values are current as of 2026 and are reviewed regularly.

faq.q: How much vitamin D should I take daily?
faq.a: The recommended daily intake is 600-800 IU, though individual needs vary by age, sun exposure, and skin type. Consult your healthcare provider for personalized recommendations.

faq.q: How long should I spend in the sun for vitamin D?
faq.a: About 15-20 minutes of midday sun exposure several times per week is generally enough for most people to produce adequate vitamin D. Fair-skinned individuals need less time than those with darker skin.
This is a guidance tool based on standard formulas. For medical diagnosis or treatment, consult a healthcare professional who can assess your individual health status.

faq.q: What are normal vitamin D levels?
faq.a: Vitamin D blood levels are typically measured as 25-hydroxyvitamin D. Normal levels range from 20-100 ng/mL, though some experts recommend levels above 30 ng/mL.

faq.q: Can I get enough vitamin D from the sun alone?
faq.a: For many people, yes—but it depends on geography, season, skin tone, age, and sun exposure habits. Sun protection and winter months may require supplementation.

faq.q: Do medications affect vitamin D levels?
faq.a: Yes, many medications including some statins, corticosteroids, and anticonvulsants can lower vitamin D levels. Always inform your doctor about your medications when discussing vitamin D.

faq.q: Should I take a vitamin D supplement?
faq.a: If you have limited sun exposure, darker skin, live in northern climates, or have certain health conditions, supplementation may be recommended. Consult your healthcare provider.

faq.q: How do age, sex, and ethnicity affect vitamin D needs?
faq.a: Vitamin D requirements and skin's ability to produce it vary significantly by age, sex, and ethnicity. Older adults may need more, and darker skin requires longer sun exposure for the same vitamin D production.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, does not require external data updates
